script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Thursday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed one point eight two percent up on the session. Semiconductors led from the front, consumer names held their ground, and a pair of cloud-adjacent heavyweights gave back ground. Here is the full picture.

Reports surfaced Thursday that China may ease restrictions on advanced Nvidia AI chip imports. That single headline moved the entire semiconductor complex. Micron surged five point six eight percent. Advanced Micro Devices climbed five point nine three percent. The ripple hit names well beyond those two, and the Nasdaq reclaimed a key technical level by the close.

Chip stocks drove broad equity gains Thursday, with the Nasdaq rallying sharply to close well above recent resistance levels. Stable weekly jobless claims reinforced the picture of labor market resilience, though Fed rate hike expectations continue to linger in the background. Investors are also beginning to rotate attention toward the upcoming earnings season and questions around valuations at current levels.

The headline of Thursday's session belongs to Micron Technology. Shares closed at nine hundred ninety-one dollars and sixty-four cents, up five point six eight percent, after the company confirmed a massive investment plan tied directly to artificial intelligence demand. That announcement dropped into a market already buzzing with reports that China could ease import restrictions on advanced Nvidia chips — a combination that proved combustible for the memory sector broadly. Analysts at multiple desks flagged Micron as a direct beneficiary of accelerating AI infrastructure buildout, specifically in high-bandwidth memory, where demand continues to outpace supply visibility. The Economic Times cited the Micron commitment as the key sentiment driver for Thursday's chip-led Nasdaq rally. For this portfolio, Micron sits at five point seven five percent of total weight — the fourth-largest position — and Thursday's move represents a meaningful contribution to the session's overall gain. The next checkpoint for Micron holders is management's formal commentary on the investment timeline and any updated guidance tied to AI memory contracts.

Nvidia closed at two hundred two dollars and seventy-eight cents, up two point nine seven percent on the session. The China chip import restriction story is the direct catalyst here. Reports indicating Beijing may ease curbs on advanced Nvidia AI chips sent the stock sharply higher in the afternoon session, with semiconductor names broadly catching a bid. Finance dot yahoo dot com noted that dip buying accelerated once those reports crossed the wire. As the portfolio's largest position at seven point six zero percent, Thursday's move in Nvidia carried significant weight in lifting the overall result. Watch the official China policy confirmation — or denial — as the next binary for this name.

Apple closed at three hundred sixteen dollars and twenty-two cents, up one point seven nine percent on the session. Two separate threads are worth noting. First, startup PrismML disclosed it has gotten a twenty-seven billion parameter AI model running on the iPhone seventeen Pro — a development that reinforces Apple's on-device AI positioning. Second, one analyst framing highlighted Apple and Nvidia as the two names with the technical foundation to lead a broader Magnificent Seven resurgence. At six point eight zero percent of the portfolio, Apple contributed solidly to Thursday's gain.

Alphabet closed at three hundred fifty-eight dollars and eighty-nine cents, down two point two two percent on the session. The negative pressure came from multiple fronts. SemiAnalysis published a report arguing that Meta is positioned to leapfrog Alphabet in frontier AI within six months, citing aggressive compute infrastructure deployment and talent acquisition. Separately, a new Meta AI model targeting agents, coding, and multimodal tasks was framed explicitly as taking aim at Alphabet and OpenAI. At six point one eight percent of the portfolio, Alphabet's decline was the single largest drag on Thursday's session result.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-four dollars and thirty-six cents, down one point one five percent. No specific news drove the move Thursday, but the pullback aligns with a broader rotation within mega-cap tech — investors moved capital toward the semiconductor complex rather than cloud software names. At four point five two percent of the portfolio, the loss was manageable. Watch upcoming earnings guidance from cloud peers for a read on Microsoft's Azure trajectory.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-seven dollars and four cents, up zero point four three percent. The session was quiet for Amazon specifically, with the modest gain reflecting general market lift rather than any company-specific catalyst. At four point zero eight percent of the portfolio, the contribution was modest but positive. The next meaningful catalyst here is the Q2 earnings release and any updated commentary on AWS growth rates.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred forty-six dollars and seventy-two cents, up five point nine three percent. That is the strongest percentage gain among primary holdings on the session. The China chip import news acted as a direct catalyst alongside Nvidia, and the dip-buying wave that swept through semiconductor names carried Advanced Micro Devices sharply higher in the afternoon. At three point eight three percent of the portfolio, Thursday's move punched above its weight in terms of contribution. The roadmap for next-generation AI accelerators remains the ongoing fundamental story.

Tesla closed at four hundred six dollars and fifty-five cents, up zero point nine one percent on the session. No company-specific news drove the move Thursday. The gain tracks with a broadly constructive tape for consumer discretionary names. At three point zero nine percent of the portfolio, the contribution was modest. The next catalyst to monitor is any updated delivery data or commentary around the autonomous vehicle product timeline.

Intel closed at one hundred twelve dollars and fifty-four cents, up one point nine five percent. The semiconductor sector tailwind from the China chip import report provided a broad lift, and Intel participated, though it remains a smaller beneficiary of AI infrastructure spending compared to peers. At two point nine zero percent of the portfolio, Thursday's move was constructive. The key watch item for Intel is any updated foundry progress report and whether management revises its AI chip roadmap.

Before closing out, three specific data points to track in the next forty-eight hours. First, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company reports earnings Friday morning — the results and forward guidance will set the tone for the entire semiconductor complex and directly frame the Nvidia, Micron, and Advanced Micro Devices positions. Second, SK Hynix begins trading on the Nasdaq Friday, a high-visibility event for the AI memory trade and a direct read on investor appetite for the sector Micron occupies. Third, Delta Air Lines also reports earnings Friday — not a direct holding, but a macro confidence signal on consumer spending that feeds into the Amazon and Tesla thesis.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. The Taiwan Semiconductor earnings release Friday morning is the single most important near-term data point for this portfolio — watch the AI chip demand commentary closely.
